title: Uncertainty-Guided Exploration for Efficient AlphaZero Training
abstract: AlphaZero has achieved remarkable success in complex decision-making problems through self-play and neural network training. However, its self-play process remains inefficient due to limited exploration of high-uncertainty positions, the overlooked runner-up decisions in Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS), and high variance in value labels. To address these challenges, we propose and evaluate uncertainty-guided exploration by branching from high-uncertainty positions using our proposed Label Change Rate (LCR) metric, which is further refined by a Bayesian inference framework. Our proposed approach leverages runner-up MCTS decisions to create multiple variations, and ensembles value labels across these variations to reduce variance. We investigate three key design parameters for our branching strategy: where to branch, how many variations to branch, and which move to play in the new branch. Our empirical findings indicate that branching with 10 variations per game provides the best performance-exploration balance. Overall, our end-to-end results show an improved sample efficiency over the baseline by 58.5\% on 9x9 Go in the early stage of training and by 47.3\% on 19x19 Go in the late stage of training.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The authors propose an uncertainty-guided MCTS exploration framework that improves upon standard AlphaZero. It branches out from high-uncertainty positions and explores multiple variations. This reduces the variance of the value label by ensembling multiple game outcomes. It also explores critical decision points that may yield improvements. This results in greater training efficiency.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
The experimental results look promising. I suggest adding error bars to some of the Figures (such as 6b, 7b, 8a, and 8b). These can be obtained by running multiple trials. Overall, the idea of uncertainty-guided exploration during MCTS makes a lot of sense.

Page-by-page comments and suggestions:

Page 1:

"It learns through self-play" No need for italics and boldface here.

"alternative fashion" → "alternating fashion"

"the strength" → "the agent's playing strength"

Page 2:

"change decisions after the tree search" Unclear what this means. Change from the policy head's prediction? If so, state so.

"during self-play while" Add comma before while.

Reduce the indentation of the contribution list.

"our analysis on Bernoulli distribution" → "our analysis of/on the Bernoulli distribution"

"in an analytical form" → "in an analytical way" / "via an analytical form" ?

Page 3:

"policy and value to obtain the search result, typically requiring hundreds to thousands
of neural network predictions to obtain a search result." Repetition of "to obtain a/the search result".

"red arrow in the figure" → "red arrow in Figure 2"

Page 4:

"In this section, we... in Section 4.1" Redundant to say "in section X" twice.

"denoted by θ = LCR(s)" Put LCR in upright font for consistency.

Lines 141-143 are a run-on sentence. Split into at least two sentences.

Page 5:

Algorithm 1: "has been played" should not be italicized.

Algorithm 1: "play_episode_from" consider using normal text and font (as opposed to monospace with underscores) for consistency with the rest of the presentation of the algorithm.

"and thus can reduce the label variance" doesn't grammatically fit with the rest of the sentence. Consider making it a new sentence: "This reduces the label variance".

Page 6:

"where LCR0 represents" Put LCR in upright font for consistency.

"so that we can discard positions whose win rates fluctuate around the initial win rate" Unclear what this means. What precisely do you mean by "fluctuate around"? Consider rephrasing or elaborating.

"positions are weighted by their time step because we want to capture the turning point in the later game phase" Unclear what "capture the turning point" means here. Consider clarifying.

"when G number of states are collected, and this hyperparameter is the same as..." → "when G states are collected, where G is the same hyperparameter as..."

"In this section, ... in Section 5.1" Redundant.

"Each training" → "Each training run"

Page 7:

"analytical estimators with" Add comma before "with".

Page 8:

"similar to our method" → "similarly to our method"

Page 9:

"our method achieves equivalent strength" Equivalent to what? Standard AlphaZero? Clarify.

"allow the return to a specific state" → "allows the algorithm to return to a specific state", if that's what you meant.

QUESTIONS:
Generalization to the non-Bernoulli case: In the Discussion section, you allude to the possibility of using quantiles. Is there any obstruction to obtaining a similar closed form for common distributions, like Gaussian or Beta distributions?

Is it possible to add uncertainty bands / error bars to some figures (such as Figures 6b, 7b, 8a, and 8b)? I assume that these can be obtained by running multiple trials and using a metric such as standard error, or a confidence interval.

Can this approach be extended to n-player games, as in Multiplayer AlphaZero?

LIMITATIONS:
Yes (page 9)
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper addresses an important and practical problem: enhancing exploration efficiency in AlphaZero's training process. The authors correctly point out the inefficiency inherent in AlphaZero’s traditional approach of initiating self-play games from the starting position every time, leaving many high-uncertainty states under-explored.

To resolve this issue, the authors introduce a well-defined uncertainty estimation metric called "Label Change Rate (LCR)." LCR intuitively and effectively quantifies state uncertainty by calculating the variability of outcomes from the same position across multiple game plays. The authors further solidify this approach through a robust Bayesian inference framework. This method clearly identifies the core of the problem and serves as a strong baseline.

The experimental design is thorough: initially validating the effectiveness and accuracy of LCR, then analyzing the impact of different branching strategies and positions on exploration efficiency, and finally demonstrating the benefits through comprehensive end-to-end training evaluations. Overall, the experimental results are convincing, showing that the proposed uncertainty-guided exploration method significantly improves AlphaZero’s training efficiency and performance.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:
Mentioned in the summary. 

Weakness:
- Using random selection as the baseline for branching is weak, as obviously certain positions should not be explored from.

- There is no direct comparison with the MCTS value baseline. Theoretically, MCTS values themselves inherently capture uncertainty effectively.

- The authors only consider uncertainty in terms of value estimation but neglect uncertainty related to policy decisions. For instance, methods such as those described in the "Learning to Stop" paper might be more appropriate, especially since agents can directly simulate outcomes. Furthermore, many states, such as the initial state, can remain persistently uncertain in value, regardless of the number of simulations performed.

- The authors do not explore alternative uncertainty estimation methods sufficiently. For example, if MCTS value remains stable throughout the search of a state, it may indicate inherent certainty in the uncertainty itself rather than genuine uncertainty about the position. Conversely, fluctuating values might signify genuine uncertainty and an area needing further training.

- The chosen threshold (lcr_0=0.48) appears arbitrary. A more principled approach, such as selecting the top 10% most uncertain positions, might yield better performance. Additionally, the optimal number of variations (V=10) was determined empirically without providing deeper insights or theoretical justifications for why this specific number is particularly effective.

QUESTIONS:
Distribution of LCR-Uncertain States:
Could the authors provide an analysis of how the LCR-uncertain states are distributed across different game stages (e.g., early, mid, late game)? Understanding this distribution could offer insights into which game phases most benefit from the proposed branching strategy.

Impact of MCTS Simulation Counts on LCR Accuracy:
It would be valuable to investigate how the accuracy of the LCR metric varies with different MCTS simulation counts. Considering recent methods frequently utilize fewer simulations (e.g., 100-200), compared to traditional settings (e.g., 800), evaluating LCR across varying simulation counts could validate its robustness and practical applicability.

Clarification on RMSE Analysis Methodology:
The current RMSE evaluation approach is not entirely clear. A suitable method might involve first obtaining multiple simulation results (like geting n=1000 z values) for each of the 20,000 positions, calculating their average as the ground truth, and then comparing this ground truth with estimates obtained from varying numbers of simulations (from 1 up to V=10). Could the authors clarify or consider revising their RMSE analysis accordingly?

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper proposes a novel uncertainty-guided exploration of positions in self-play for AlphaZero. Using a proposed Label Change Rate (LCR) metric, positions with high uncertainty are identified after self-play trajectories are generated.  The LCR values are used to sample an uncertain state, and a new action is sampled from the policy in that state, generating a new trajectory. This is repeated V times, and all the generated trajectories are used to estimate values of states and as training data for the Neural Network training phase of AlphaZero. Experimental results show the accuracy of the LCR metric, and the sample efficiency gains of the proposed method, in two variants of Go.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths: 
- The paper was generally clear and well-written.  
- The proposed idea is simple, and to my knowledge original, but shows clear promise in the experimental results. 
- The evaluation experiments were well-designed and clear. I liked the methodology used to determine the best V value, as an example.

Weaknesses: 
- Perhaps it was just me, but it took a second pass through the paper to grasp the overall idea of what was happening in the paper, what the main idea actually was.  I think a lot of this was due to a misunderstanding of how some of the terminology (ie "variations") was being used.  I think that could have been made a bit more clear. 
- It seems that a few more alternatives could have been included in the experimental results, beyond simply the baseline and random selection of branching points.  For example, uniformly sampling among branch points with LCR above threshold, instead of utilizing the time step of the move to bias towards later in the game. 
- It is unclear how some of the decisions made, like V=10, would translate to other domains beyond Go.

QUESTIONS:
1. I was a bit surprised that the experiment in section 5.2 only included two independent game plays from each position. Would the results change if more game plays were performed, getting a more accurate estimate of LCR for each position, but perhaps sacrificing the number of positions that could be averaged over?

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper tackles a long-standing inefficiency in AlphaZero-style self-play: most positions are visited only once and the single game result used as a training target is noisy.

The authors propose **Uncertainty-Guided Branching (UGB)**:

- Label Change Rate – an analytic, closed-form estimate of how likely the game outcome would flip if the same position were replayed. A Bayesian update refines LCR when repeated roll-outs are available.
- Where / how / what to branch – positions whose posterior LCR exceeds a threshold are selected; 10 runner-up MCTS moves are replayed from each such position; their outcomes are ensembled to reduce value-label variance.
- Extensive experiments on 9 × 9 and 19 × 19 Go shows an improved sample efficiency over the baseline by 58.5% on 9x9 Go in the early stage of training and by 47.3% on 19x19 Go in the late stage of training.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
## Strengths

- The paper is well structured with core ideas explained.

- Tackles an important inefficiency problem in Go self-play.

## Weaknesses

- GPU-hour counts are given, but end-to-end wall-time and memory impact of branching are not reported.

- Branching change the visited distribution; effect on final policy optimality vs exploration bias is not analized.

QUESTIONS:
1. Could the authors provide wall-clock training time and per-move latency overhead relative to the baseline?
2. How robust is performance to the LCR threshold or to choosing 5 vs 10 branches when total compute is fixed?
3. An ablation without the Bayesian update would clarify its added value?
4. The Elo ratings for both baseline and Ours are still small on Figure 8 (b). Could the authors provide a figure with more training samples?

LIMITATIONS:
See questions.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (poster)

COMMENT:
All reviewers recommend acceptance and praise the clear writing and well designed experiments. I follow their recommendation and also recommend acceptance.

Reviewer gKQg made some valid observations about the type of uncertainty (e.g. from policy decisions, which the authors acknowledged) and asked for additional baselines that the authors promised to include. The authors are reminded to include these discussions and experiments in the final version of the paper. 

Upon reading the paper myself, I noted that the related work section is missing a related branch of literature about planning to explore (listed blow). The objective is not directly comparable to the method presented in this paper, but appear related enough to the core problem to be mentioned.

**Additional references on planning to explore**

Yi Sun, Faustino J. Gomez, and Jurgen Schmidhuber. Planning to be surprised: Optimal Bayesian exploration in dynamic environments. Artificial General Intelligence - 4th International Conference, 2011.

Pranav Shyam, Wojciech Jaskowski, and Faustino Gomez. Model-based active exploration. Proceedings of the 36th International Conference on Machine Learning, 2019.

Ramanan Sekar, Oleh Rybkin, Kostas Daniilidis, Pieter Abbeel, Danijar Hafner, and Deepak Pathak. Planning to explore via self-supervised world models. Proceedings of the 37th International Conference on Machine Learning, 2020.

Carlos E. Luis, Alessandro G. Bottero, Julia Vinogradska, Felix Berkenkamp, and Jan Peters. Model-based uncertainty in value functions. International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics, 2023.

Yaniv Oren, Viliam Vadocz, Matthijs T. J. Spaan, and Wendelin Bohmer. Epistemic Monte Carlo Tree Search. International Conference on Learning Representations, 2025.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
